https://github.com/Zettlr/Zettlr/commit/.commit_message: feat: Move openPaths to openFiles and openWorkspaces (#6080)

ranked_scenarios.summary: feat: refactor FSAL to separate file tree and abstraction logic

description.text:
This PR is now ready for review.

I have also tagged you, @sensologica, in one place where you can, if you want to, start thinking about nice ways of implementing that (search for your name below).

## Description

This PR fulfills a somewhat older dream of mine: The actual creation of a _File System Abstraction Layer_.

Some background: When I wrote the FSAL, I intended to create a class that would abstract any file access away from the application logic, in a somewhat similar way as Laravel's (or Symfony's?) storage providers work.

The main idea was simple: Centralize all file system logic in one place, and locate the business logic someplace else. This would also give me the chance to easily support remote file systems, such as WebDav, Dropbox, Google Drive, or One Drive (if I at some point in my life find the time to do that lol).

Long story short: I wanted to call `loadFile(filePath)` somewhere in my app, and the `fs.readFile`-stuff would happen in the FSAL.

Now, that was my goal. Which I missed. By about a mile. The thing is, back when I implemented the FSAL, the app was not quite ready, since I had two problems:

1. fsevents didn't work back then ([read here](https://www.hendrik-erz.de/post/electron-chokidar-and-native-nodejs-modules-a-horror-story-from-integration-hell) on the entire sh\*\*hole that was the fsevents integration).
2. I had no place where I would collect and manage the file tree.

Problem (1) led to a ton of arbitrary logic of patching the file tree in numerous creative ways. The issue was that I didn't trust fsevents (and myself) back then. This led to all the `ignoreEvents` shenanigans that have, in my humble opinion, led to bloated code in the FSAL, because especially the `move` action required a ton of additional code and absurd amounts of events being broadcasted across the entire app.

Problem (2) led to me actually violating the FSAL name: Instead of just abstracting away the file system logic, I also stuffed the entire file tree management logic in there. Which led to even more bloat.

In effect, these two problems I faced back in the day led to a class that is currently well above 1,000 lines of code and that has tons of problems. These problems mainly stem from the insufficient edge case handling in the brittle patching logic that I had thus far. Users oftentimes complain (and rightly so) that some events just don't get processed by the app and in the worst case, users would need to restart the app.

I had the honor once when I checked out an older commit in a loaded git repository. Git's moving back and forth of tons of files has just defeated the state management in the under-equipped FSAL.

With this PR, I hope to address most of these issues. It is on the one hand a refactor, and on the other hand an entire reconceptualization of file management in the app.

## Changes

On a high level, this PR changes only two things:

1. Remove the entire logic of file tree management out of the FSAL and into their own classes (`Root`). This entirely separates FS management and file tree management logic.
2. Move the entire responsibility of actually detecting changes to the chokidar library, and not tamper with anything it does.

In addition, this PR has a series of very neat side effects:

1. This PR separates all loaded roots from each other. This will make implementing a very nice feature easy: Enable users to use windows as a form of "Workspaces" similar to VSCode, where they can choose which folders they see, and which not. I myself figured out that I really want to visually separate my reading notes and Zettelkasten directories (where I am not interested in browsing files inasmuch as I want to search them) from my PhD directory (where everything follows a set folder structure), so that's now a possibility. **Possibly interesting for @sensologica.**
2. This logic is inspired a lot from CodeMirror's new collab plugin and makes it easy to manage file trees in any combination
3. **This PR begins the switch to Pinia as already suggested years ago by @tobiasdiez.** The new logic has been entirely implemented in Pinia stores which, for now, live side by side with the Vuex store. This makes the entire logic in the renderer type safe and enables us to at some point get rid of the very last JavaScript-file in the project (`item-mixin.js`).
4. This PR now centralizes all file system logic. Specifically, I took the liberty of going across the application and redirecting any arbitrary file access from direct access of the  `fs` module to the FSAL. There are a few places where `isDir` and `isFile` still need to be called, but those are comparatively safe now. This reduces the amount of synchronous file access to almost zero across the runtime of the app.

## Additional information

I just want to highlight how insanely simple implementation was. This goes to show how simple programming can be if your data model is appropriate and fit to the task. For years, I was grappling with implementing billions of state changes, but implementing the queue for file tree changes was a breeze. Thanks at this point to the anonymous redditor, who recommended "Threadsafe queues" to me, which I didn't end up implementing but which gave me the final inspiration to pull through with this PR.

* Closes #4337

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: macOS Sonoma 14.1.1 (MBP 14'' M2 Pro)

## Known Bugs

I have now merged the current develop branch with all the changes into the PR, and re-tested the things. I've discovered a few more tiny bugs:

- [x] Editing a file externally messes up the affected main editor, which doesn't properly (re)load its theme
- [x] Editing a file externally twice messes up the loaded document in the document authority (Note: After the first editing, I ticked the "Always load changes" checkbox; have to verify this)
- [x] Renaming a file externally closes the file in the document tree but doesn't close a potentially now-empty pane, despite that it should (i.e. there is a pane with a single file open -> rename it externally -> Zettlr will close the file as expected -> Zettlr will, however, not close the corresponding pane that is now empty)

closed_issues.summary: Bugs in the FSAL updating process

description.text:
### Description

Sometimes – especially when modifying many different files via the Explorer/Finder/file browser (i.e., remotely from the perspective of Zettlr), the file tree that is displayed in the file manager is no longer in sync with what is actually on disk.

### Reproducing

Zettlr is running. Then you create a new folder in any of the loaded workspaces in Zettlr and in it put a dozen different files, and afterward you rename those files, one by one. Then, when you go back to the Zettlr file manager to check what it displays, you notice that it hasn't updated everything. So what Zettlr displays you is not necessarily what is actually on disk.

### Zettlr Version

Right off the develop branch

### Specify version

v3.0.0-beta.4-develop

### Installation Method

None

### Your Platform

- [ ] Windows
- [X] macOS
- [ ] Linux

### Architecture

- [ ] Intel 64bit
- [X] ARM 64bit

### Operating System Version

macOS Ventura 13.2.1

### Additional Information

I believe this bug to be partially related to how we manage updates in the renderer process. Currently, it looks like this:

1. Something changes remotely
2. The FSAL notices this and updates its own file tree
3. It records events of everything added, changed, or deleted
4. It sends a notification to the renderer process that something has changed
5. The renderer then requests all events (additions, changes, deletions) that happened since the last time it asked and attempts to apply them one by one

I believe that in step 5, sometimes there are still problems since sometimes files get added and then immediately deleted, which make it difficult for the logic to keep up.

I believe that a better logic should be found that isn't as brittle as the current one. Specifically, I am currently pondering of whether the following would be sufficient (especially in terms of speed):

1. Something changes remotely
2. The FSAL notices this and updates its own file tree
3. It sends a notification to the renderer process of the minimal subtree it has to reload to be back in sync
4. The renderer requests this minimal subtree and exchanges it in its own representation
5. Should that fail, the renderer attempts a full recovery of the workspace in question

This would ensure that the renderer is not always requesting the entire tree (which can be Megabytes in size) but rather the minimum necessity without being too granular. A subtree should only in worst cases take noticeable time to get changed, a workspace will lead to a small delay, but I think that would be worth it, especially given that the FSAL has always had these synchronization problems.

<!-- Thank you for opening up this pull request! Please make sure to fill out as
much information as you can below.

But, most importantly, please make sure you can say "I did so!" to the
following points:

  - [ ] I documented all behaviour as far as I could do.
  - [ ] I target the develop-branch, and *not* the master branch.
  - [ ] I have tested this extensively and paid extra attention to
        potential cross-platform issues (e.g. Cmd/Ctrl/Super-key bindings)
  - [ ] I have made use of ESLint using the provided configuration from the
        repository's .eslintrc.json file, and it did not complain.
  - [ ] I have added an entry to the CHANGELOG.md.
  - [ ] I matched my code-style to the repository (as far as possible).
  - [ ] I do agree that my code will be published under the GNU GPL v3 license.
  - [ ] As far as JS-files are concerned, I made sure to copy (in case of new files)
        or adapt (in case of existing files) the info in the header.
  - [ ] I synced the latest commits to develop shortly before proposing
        so that no merge issues occur.

  N.B.: Of course you can open a Pull Request and ask for certain things
  such as file structure later on! It does not need to be perfect on the first
  try :)
 -->

## Description
As described in #1977, upgrading from older versions throws the error `TypeError: replacements is not iterable` since back then the autocorrect replacements were stored in a different format. This is fixed. 

## Changes
<!-- What changes did you make? Please explicitly state any breaking API changes so that nobody is confused why other components suddenly stop working -->

## Additional information
<!-- If there is anything else that might be of interest, please provide it here -->

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: Win 10

## Description

This PR aims to fully rewrite the entire FSAL a second time. This time, the goal is to get rid of any brittle tree structures that we essentially do not need, and instead treat the entire file system as a long list, and defer any tree-like recursion to the Vue renderer. Because that's the only time we actually need a tree: when we display the file tree.

The reason for this is simple: When I wrote the first file system layer back in 2017, I had zero plan of how all of this works, and so I essentially mistrusted anything, and kept every descriptor I needed always loaded.

When I rewrote the layer the first time to create the FSAL, I went a bit bolder, but still decided to keep an entire file system tree in memory. And not just that, there's copies of that file system tree across all main windows. You can imagine how much memory that eats up.

This time, the goal is to treat the filesystem as the single source of truth, and only load from disk what we absolutely need. Any changes during runtime will be deferred to chokidar, which now works flawlessly since a few years.

By doing so, I hope to achieve the following goals:

* Get rid of any nested tree structures that are difficult to navigate and brittle to patch
* Only load what is absolutely necessary for the functioning
* By getting rid of any trees, I can treat the loaded files as a single large list wherever necessary, which makes dealing with it much simpler. I'm thinking of the tag provider, the link provider, and anything else that extracts information from this tree.
* Get rid of the ugly recursion in the FSAL that requires instantiating and passing around an unwieldy Markdown file parser that, en passent, also doesn't work properly.
* Improve performance considerably, both in terms of CPU time, simply because there's less work to do, and in terms of memory consumption, because there's no weird trees hanging around.
* Make the code much simpler by removing all the bloated tree-patching functionality.
* Fix issues pertaining to the app not being able to pick up on all changes that happen on the file system.
* Bypass all providers and grab the necessary information directly from the renderer via the FSAL.

## Changes

* change!: Completely get rid of the brittle tree structures. Instead, everywhere directories are treated as simple lists, and only the renderers will, where necessary, recursively treat them as trees for visual purposes.
* change!: Rewrite the `workspaces`-pinia store in the renderer. It now only keeps a list of all loaded paths, and all descriptors, patching these lists as FSAL events come in with no tree merging.
* change!: Completely remove the `WorkspaceProvider`.
* refactor: The file system watchers now report all events in raw form, and the FSAL will immediately broadcast them. This interestingly has led to the updating of file items in the file manager to be much faster and feel almost instant.
* chore: Removed a large amount of old, dead code in the directory module (`FSALDir`) that hasn't been in use.
* Many commands are now much leaner, since they essentially just trigger raw filesystem actions instead of going through the bloated tree update procedures.
* change!: No recursive file tree reading on parsing directories. This means that **`DirDescriptor`s no longer have a `children` property**! Instead, anyone who needs the children of a folder needs to make a second round trip to the main process. There is only one instance where an entire file tree will be read in. This is only meant so that we have a list of all loaded files (for the file autocomplete, for example), and then it will only completely read in the paths of the file tree, not its descriptors.
* change: Directory sorting is now completely deferred to each renderer element. Every `TreeItem` and the `FileList` will now sort its own children (recursively in the case of the `FileList`) according to general and particular sorting rules as defined in the settings and on the directory. This fixes a bunch of sorting issues we have had.
* fix: Small but until now unnoticed bug where the config provider would never emit an update event when the `openPaths` (=roots/workspaces) have changed.
* feat: Debounce splash screen update to at most 60fps from the main process to not overload the IPC pipe with large amounts of updates. (The splash screen now sees all individual files as they are being re-indexed by the FSAL.)
* chore: Improved typing of some file system commands (the `arg` property).
* refactor: Rename `obj` --> `item` in both `TreeItem` and `FileItem`.

## Additional information

### Fixes:

#5938
#5920
#5784
#5773
#5594
#5184
#5345

(if you find more, feel free to ping me!)

### Still TODO:

- [x] Proper sorting of children in the file list (essentially here we need a recursive array sorter)
- [x] Some more testing
- [x] A few performance tests and improvements

### Caveats

There are some performance bottlenecks, but fortunately these only seem to come from the fact that I perform a few too many IPC calls. So by now (at least on my relatively fast computer) the biggest performance bottleneck seems to be the IPC pipe which works much better with larger chunks of data.

Tested on: macOS 26

closed_issues.summary: Directory > Properties > Sort by time: does not auto refresh

description.text:
### Description

In the ideal situation the working repository sidebar folder view with `Properties` set to `Sort by time` would automatically update.

e.g. saving a file would move it to the top if `Sort by time` and `ascending` is selected in `Properties`.

Instead, the order does not refresh until right-click repository in working area to bring up `Properties` dialogue. 

### Reproducing

Save new version of file.

File does not automatically change its timed sort order in `Repositories` sidebar folder view.

### Zettlr version

3.6.0

### Installation Method

From the Website or GitHub

### Installation Method (Other)

_No response_

### Your Platform

- [x] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Linux

### Architecture

- [x] Intel 64bit
- [ ] ARM 64bit (includes Apple Silicon)

### Operating System Version

Windows 11 Pro 24H2

### Additional Information

_No response_

## Description
<!-- Below, please describe what the PR does in one or two short sentences. -->
This PR fixes all of the remaining type warnings.

## Changes
<!-- What changes did you make? Please explicitly state any breaking API changes so that nobody is confused why other components suddenly stop working -->
A lot of type casting, mostly `as string`.

The `NodeError` type was exported.

## Additional information
<!-- If there is anything else that might be of interest, please provide it here -->
There are no more type warnings or lint errors :)

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: <!-- OS including version --> macOS 26

## Description

This is the PR I'll continuisly keep updated in order to close #3063

## Changes

This PR has one job: To get rid of the `globals` and side-effects pertaining to these. In order to do these, the following changes have been made (checkboxes are ticked):

- [x] Create an `AppServiceContainer` which serves as a singleton class that can inject service providers into other service providers
- [x] Move all provider instantiations into the app service container which is launched and shut down just like the service providers before in the lifecycle functions
- [x] Inject providers on a need-be basis to the providers, that is: all receive an instance of the logger, the rest depending on their needs
- [x] Refactored the `WindowManager` into the `WindowProvider`
- [x] Fixed a race condition in which the window provider and the tray provider required each other simultaneously
- [x] Moved the loading of the language data into the translation provider, where it should've been for years now
- [x] Refactor the `DocumentManager` into the `DocumentProvider`
- [x] Refactor the `Zettlr` class into a `CommandProvider`
- [x] Completely remove all `globals` in the main process
- [x] Clean up the `global.d.ts` and remove any provider typings as possible, since the types are now propagated correctly via dependency injection
- [x] Clean the absurd dependency hell that has just unearthed in the FSAL
- [ ] Find a solution to providing the language data via the `globals`, since that is something I have not yet figured out without having every file require the translation provider (or is that feasible?)
- [x] The md2html converter depends on the citation provider, we need to inject that both in main and in the renderer

## Additional information
<!-- If there is anything else that might be of interest, please provide it here -->

I did some quick testing and thus far the app starts without all too much hassle. However, I still don't trust the linter via ESLint vis-a-vis VSCode's linting vis-a-vis the typechecking during builds, since they give me mixed messages. Feel free to have additional looks at this.

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: macOS 12 (Monterey), arm64 arch, but this PR should be platform independent

***

CC @tobiasdiez @kyaso — this PR is not yet done so you don't have to do any deep code review just now. Just feel free to keep an eye out on this PR, and feel free to suggest improvements if you see I'm running into the wrong direction somewhere.

closed_issues.summary: [DEVELOP] Migrate main modules to service providers

description.text:
I'm opening this issue to have a communication channel with the rest of the community regarding a large refactoring I've begun.

Until now, there was this distinction between `modules` and `service providers`, where modules were roughly equivalent to simple Javascript modules (i.e. you have a folder with an index and in there resides the module) and service providers were modules which did not expose any access to other parts of the code. The only code which had direct access to the service providers is the lifecycle hook which boots them up and shuts them down. Access to the internal state of these modules was always facilitated using the `global` object (inside the main process) and the `broadcastIpcMessage` function (towards the renderer processes).

While that was a cool thing, the main reason I used the `global` object was both because module support in an early version of Node was not that good and simply because I honestly didn't understand how modules work.

Over the past two years, however, the whole setup of the app has changed quite considerably. That has had several reasons:

* First, I should name the efforts by the Electron team to mitigate security issues with Electron apps, among these deactivation of node integration, context isolation, and other features which were slowly included. This means that the renderers are now fully autonomous and we could – if we provide the correct APIs – just take some of the renderer process and display it as-is on a website.
* Then, @tobiasdiez has introduced TypeScript to the codebase, and, alongside with that, webpack, electron-forge and thus a host of new possibilities. Ever since his large PR was merged, the files are now combined, minimized and otherwise optimized.
* Furthermore, I lost my fear that something bad could happen in the main process. Or, rather: I now know how to keep code safe without locking everything up.

Additionally to these reasons, there are now new demands for the application: Progress is halting, mainly because code that I would like to implement doesn't have access to some of the codebase which is necessary to implement this simple. As a very palpable example, take the demand for a network visualization of the files: While we now do have a link provider, that link provider is currently unable to resolve the outlinks into correct path names. The functionality for that, however, is already there: the `findExact` method of the FSAL. However, since that is only accessible by the main Zettlr object, the link provider has no means to resolve those links.

In fact, the main Zettlr class is the last remnant from the early versions prior to 1.0 where I organized everything OOP, an hommage to my background with Laravel and PHP in general, where OOP is much more prevalent than in the JavaScript world.

Given that background, here's what I am currently trying to do:

1. Refactor the service providers from classes to functional components which maintain a module-wide state and expose functions which allow limited access to that state in a safe manner. This will not only remove our reliance on the global object, but additionally make typing much easier, since we can simply type the functions and thus remove a lot of boilerplate from the type files (which I already migrated into a new `types` directory)
2. Convert the FSAL, the DocumentManager, and the Importer and Exporter to service providers, that is: Functional modules residing on the top level of the app. This will then additionally end the distinction between service providers and modules: The main process will then only have service providers. The renderers will still have modules, but that is because there are multiple of them, and for example the Markdown editor is something that's needed in several places
3. After this has been done, one of my next goals would be if I can safely remove the `zettlr.ts` class file. I don't yet know how to do this the most proper way, but I'm sure after there is no more state which the class manages a few avenues will open up.

The benefits of this whole endeavor will be that the complexity of the main process is radically reduced and we can finally begin to implement a few other neat things such as the typing of IPC modules which Tobias has already begun working on. Furthermore, this will make it easier for us to slowly migrate the whole preload module towards a way that offers a simple API. This will in turn make it easy to – some day – take the main window code, move it to an external repository, and from there on build a web application (PWA) of Zettlr, which utilizes the code in two places: The desktop app and said web app.

***

In the end, I think this is simply the logical conclusion of going from "I have no idea what I'm doing" towards someone who actually knows what a desktop app needs and how to properly structure it. All in all, it's just another technical debt in the wall!

CC because you seem to be reasonbly active in making PRs and/or thus might be interested to know about this: @kyaso @derwok @Kangie @newhinton
